reasoning: The base rate of a U.S. president visiting Russia is historically very low, with no such visit occurring since Obama in 2013. Given the current geopolitical climate and the ongoing war in Ukraine, a presidential visit to Moscow would be extremely politically and logistically controversial and complex. While Trump and Putin have expressed interest in meeting to negotiate an end to the war, diplomatic precedent and recent statements (including the Kremlin mentioning Saudi Arabia as a potential location) strongly suggest that any summit would take place in a neutral third country (e.g., Saudi Arabia, Turkey, or a European nation). Furthermore, news reports updated through May 29, 2025, indicate that Trump has been "unable to coax Russia into peace talks," implying no concrete meeting plan has been solidified. Trump has also relied on his envoy, Steve Witkoff, to travel to Moscow rather than going himself. With only one month left in the resolution window (June 2025) and no scheduled trip on the horizon, the chance of an abrupt, unannounced visit to Russia is exceptionally low.
